front: What are the five components of the Database System Environment?
back: Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures, and Users. These components work together to enable effective database management and operations.

front: Define DBMS and its primary purpose.
back: DBMS (Database Management System) is software that enables creation, maintenance, and manipulation of databases. Its primary purpose is to provide an efficient and reliable way to store, retrieve, and manage data.
The ability to change the logical schema (conceptual level) without affecting the application programs or external schema. It protects the application layer from changes in the database structure.
The ability to change the physical schema (internal level) without affecting the logical schema. Changes like storage allocation or indexing methods don't impact the user view.

front: Define the three-schema architecture in DBMS.
back: Three-schema architecture has three levels: External Schema (user view), Conceptual Schema (logical view), and Internal Schema (physical storage view). It provides data abstraction and independence.
A schema is the overall structure or blueprint of a database that defines tables, fields, relationships, and constraints. It can be at external, conceptual, or internal level.

front: What is the difference between instance and schema?
back: Schema is the structural design/blueprint of the database (static), while instance is the actual data stored at a particular time (dynamic and changes frequently).

front: What are the functions of a DBMS?
back: Key functions: Data storage/retrieval, Data manipulation, Transaction management, Concurrency control, Security management, Data integrity enforcement, and Backup/recovery.

front: What is data redundancy and why is it problematic?
back: Data redundancy is duplicate data stored in multiple places. Problems include: Wasted storage space, Data inconsistency, Update anomalies, and Increased maintenance complexity.

front: Define a data model and name common types.
back: A data model is a set of concepts for describing data, its relationships, and constraints. Common types include: Hierarchical, Network, Relational, Entity-Relationship, and Object-oriented models.

front: What is the role of the Database Administrator (DBA)?
back: DBA responsibilities include: Database design, Security management, Performance tuning, Backup/recovery, Ensuring data integrity, and Managing user access privileges.

front: What is meant by data abstraction in databases?
back: Data abstraction is the process of hiding complex implementation details while exposing only essential features. The three-schema architecture provides different levels of abstraction.

front: What is the difference between centralized and distributed database systems?
back: Centralized: Single database stored at one location accessed by multiple users. Distributed: Database stored across multiple locations connected via network, appearing as single database to users.

front: What are the advantages of using a DBMS over file processing systems?
back: Advantages: Reduced data redundancy, Improved data consistency, Better data security, Concurrent access handling, Data integrity enforcement, Efficient data recovery, and Data independence.

front: What is a transaction in DBMS context?
back: A transaction is a logical unit of work consisting of one or more database operations that are executed as a single unit. It must follow ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
